items.prompt_md: What is the purpose of the `dummy` node in the merge?
options: It holds the final answer directly, so `dummy` itself is returned
It gives `tail` a valid node to point from before any real node has been attached, avoiding a "first node" special case
It stores the total length of the merged list
It marks the boundary between the two input lists
items.answer: It gives `tail` a valid node to point from before any real node has been attached, avoiding a "first node" special case
items.explanation_md: `dummy.next` is always where the real merged list begins. `tail` starts at `dummy`, so the very first attachment is written through `tail.next` exactly like every later one.

items.prompt_md: You're merging two already-sorted singly linked lists into one sorted list by repeatedly taking the smaller head. Which approach fits without special-casing the first node attached?
options: Copy both lists into an array, sort the array, and rebuild a list
Use a dummy sentinel node before the real head, and always write through a trailing `tail` pointer
Reverse both lists first, then merge from the back
Recompute the shorter list's length before merging
items.answer: Use a dummy sentinel node before the real head, and always write through a trailing `tail` pointer
items.explanation_md: A dummy head means `tail.next` is always a valid write target, even for the very first node — no branch needed for the first attachment.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of merging two sorted linked lists of total length n with a dummy head?
options: O(n log n) time, O(1) extra space
items.explanation_md: Each of the n nodes from either list is visited and relinked exactly once; only the `dummy` and `tail` pointers are extra, and existing nodes are reused rather than copied — O(n) time, O(1) extra space.
